coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective determination of Fe3+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yulin
Mao, Linhan
Cui, Congcong
Hu, Yong
Chen, Zhaoxia
Zhan, Yuan
Zhang, Yuhong
coredata.dc:description: At present, the contamination of water resources by heavy metal ions has posed a significant threat to human survival. Therefore, it is particularly critical to develop low-cost, easy-to-use, and highly efficient heavy metal detection technologies. In this work, a fast and cost-effective fluorescent probe for nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was prepared using one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid (CA) as carbon source, and melamine as nitrogen source. The structural and optical characterizations of the resulting N-CDs were investigated in details. The results showed that the quantum yield of the prepared fluorescent probe was as high as 45 %, and an average fluorescence lifetime was about 7.80 ns. N-CDs have excellent water solubility and dispersibility, with an average size of 2.58 nm. N-CDs exhibited excellent specific responsiveness to Fe3+ and can be used as an effective method for detecting Fe3+ at low-concentrations (the concentrations of N-CDs as low as 0.24 μg/mL) using fluorescent probes. The linear response of the fluorescent probe N-CDs to Fe3+ was formed in the concentration range of 20–80 μM, and the detection limit was 3.18 μM. In addition, in the actual water samples analysis, the recovery rate reached 97.05–100.58 %. The prepared of N-CDs provide available Fe3+ fluorescent probes in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Specific responsivity
Fe3+ detection

Detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples
Quenching mechanism of N-CDs for Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) XRD curve, (c) TEM image, and (d) the lateral size distributions of N-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum, high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s spectra of N-CDs.

Optical characterizations of the specific N-CDs (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. inset shows images of N-CDs under visible (left) and UV (right) light irradiation; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (280–350 nm).

(a) Fluorescence properties of N-CDs under various pH conditions. (b) The relationship between N-CDs and various concentration NaCl solution. (c) Photostability of N-CDs under 365 nm UV irradiation for 12 h.
Specific selectivity of Fe3+ by N-CDs.
(a) Emission spectra at various Fe3+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100 μM); (b) Effect of Fe3+ concentration (20–80 μM) on F/F0.

(a) UV–vis absorption of N-CDs dispersed in various Fe3+ concentrations. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and the mixtures of N-CDs with 40 μM Fe3+. (c) Fluorescence quenching mechanism diagram of N-CDs by Fe3+.
Illustration of the preparation of N-CDs for detection Fe3+.
Comparison with CDs for the detection of Fe3+.
Quantitation of Fe3+ in water sample.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective determination of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry-of- Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, Hubei Key Laboratory for Precision Synthesis of Small Molecule Pharmaceuticals, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry-of- Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules
Hubei Key Laboratory for Precision Synthesis of Small Molecule Pharmaceuticals
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ministry-of- Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, Hubei Key Laboratory for Precision Synthesis of Small Molecule Pharmaceuticals, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: At present, the contamination of water resources by heavy metal ions has posed a significant threat to human survival. Therefore, it is particularly critical to develop low-cost, easy-to-use, and highly efficient heavy metal detection technologies. In this work, a fast and cost-effective fluorescent probe for nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was prepared using one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid (CA) as carbon source, and melamine as nitrogen source. The structural and optical characterizations of the resulting N-CDs were investigated in details. The results showed that the quantum yield of the prepared fluorescent probe was as high as 45 %, and an average fluorescence lifetime was about 7.80 ns. N-CDs have excellent water solubility and dispersibility, with an average size of 2.58 nm. N-CDs exhibited excellent specific responsiveness to Fe3+ and can be used as an effective method for detecting Fe3+ at low-concentrations (the concentrations of N-CDs as low as 0.24 μg/mL) using fluorescent probes. The linear response of the fluorescent probe N-CDs to Fe3+ was formed in the concentration range of 20–80 μM, and the detection limit was 3.18 μM. In addition, in the actual water samples analysis, the recovery rate reached 97.05–100.58 %. The prepared of N-CDs provide available Fe3+ fluorescent probes in the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ions (Fe3+) play an extremely important role in living organisms, participating in multiple biological processes from oxygen metabolism to DNA imprinting, RNA transcription, protein synthesis, and enhancing the oxygen transport capacity of hemoglobin [1]. As we all known, insufficient Fe3+ in the body may cause anemia, and excess Fe3+ can lead to damage to the pancreas, liver, kidneys, and other functional organs, such as cirrhosis, kidney failure, and heart disease [2]. Therefore, sensitive and accurate Fe3+ detection is particularly important in the environment and humans. Currently, there are various methods available for detecting of metal ions, such as ion chromatography [3], coupled plasma spectroscopy [4], and electrochemical analysis [5]. These methods have some advantages such as high sensitivity and selectivity, but they also have drawbacks such as requiring large instruments and complex operating processes, which greatly limits their practical application. In recent years, the fluorescent probe technique has received much attention due to its simple operation, cost-effectiveness, high selectivity, rapid response time, and ease of monitoring [6]. At present, the main probe materials used to detect Fe3+ include semiconductor quantum dots [7], organic fluorescent dyes [8], and metal–organic frameworks [9]. However, these materials also suffer from the disadvantages of high elemental toxicity, low sensitivity, low selectivity, and insufficient water solubility and photostability confine their universal applications. Therefore, developing green, rapid, convenient, and cost-effective new probes for the detection of Fe3+ is particularly urgent.

Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nanomaterial with fluorescent properties and a diameter less than 10 nm [10], which have the advantages of simple preparation of high photoluminescence [11], chemical tenability [12], low toxicity [13] and high biocompatibility [14]. Due to their advantages, CDs can be widely used in photocatalysis [15], fluorescent probes [16], biological imaging [17], drug-carrying therapeutics in organisms [18], analytical detection [19], organic molecules [20], and proteins [21]. Among them, CDs-based fluorescence probes for the detection of various metal ions like Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+ and Al3+ have received widespread attention [22–24]. Especially, CDs are suitable for specific detection of metal ions through electrostatic or coordination interactions [25]. Zhang et al. developed a N-doped carbon dots dual-signal sensing system for efficient detection of Cu2+ through intermolecular electrostatic interactions [26]. Yang et al. reported the synthesis of biomass-related red fluorescence CDs and applied it for determination of Hg2+ based on the coordination of Hg2+ with surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs [27]. Undoubtedly, these CDs fluorescent sensors were beneficial to the detection of metal ions. However, the majority of these reported CDs were limited to the weaker fluorescence intensity and lower quantum yield, resulting in higher concentrations of CDs used in actual ion detection and restricting their further development [22,28–30]. It is extremely necessary to explore simple and rapid techniques for synthesizing CDs with lower concentrations to effectively select and sensitively detect metal ions.

Herein, a kind of green, low-cost, and high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were carried out by a one-step hydrothermal process using citric acid (CA) and melamine as precursors and water as the solvent (Scheme 1 ). As a weak organic acid, citric acid has the advantages of safety, non-toxicity, and low cost, making it an ideal carbon source [31]. Melamine with good stability was selected as the nitrogen source for doping citric acid to improve the photoluminescence performance of CDs [32]. The structure, optical characteristics, and morphological appearance of N-CDs were analyzed carefully, and the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ was preliminarily investigated. A simple, high sensitive, low concentration, and wide detection range fluorescent probe for ferric ions detection was established, and the application of N-CDs for Fe3+ test in actual water samples was investigated. It provides a simple method for metal ions detection by the fluorescent probe with broad application prospect in the field of sensing.

Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5 %), citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 38 %), and ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 98 %) were obtained from Sinopharm (China). Melamine (C3H6N6, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 95 %), quinine sulfate (C9H7·H2SO4N, 95 %), and acetate buffer (ABS, pH = 7.4) were purchased from Macklin (China).

The fluorescence intensity was obtained by using a fluorescence spectrometer (LS-55, Perkin-Elmer) with a xenon pulsed lamp. The quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was calculated by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensity (excited at 320 nm) using quinine sulfate (QY = 54 %) as a reference[25]. N-CDs size distribution images were obtained using a JEOL JEM 2100F field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The optical characterizations of N-CDs were investigated by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu Corp, Tokyo, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the sample was obtained in the range 500–4000 cm−1 on an infrared spectrophotometer (IS10, Nicolet). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded using the Escalab 250Xi spectrometer to analyze the elements and bonds. Crystallographic information was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation.

N-CDs was synthesized by a cost-effective and simple one-step hydrothermal process. Briefly, CA (0.67 g) and melamine (1.33 g) were ultrasonically dissolved in 15 mL deionized water solution. The solution was poured into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and continuously heated at 180 °C in air oven for 10 h. Then, the reactor was naturally cooled to ambient temperature. The obtained pale yellow carbon dots solution was separated in a centrifuge for 20 min at a speed of 10,000 rpm at 25 °C. The resulting N-CDs solution was dialyzed (MWCO, 1000 Da) for 24 h with water to eliminate impurities and maintained at 4 °C for further investigation.

The stability of the fluorescence N-CDs was investigated under UV irradiation (365 nm, Dark box type UV lamp) for 12 h. The impact of different NaCl concentrations (0–1.0 mol/L) on the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs was explored. The pH stability was analyzed by adjusting pH (1.0–13.0) with 0.1 mol/L of NaOH and HCl solution. All the stability tests were performed and recorded at 320 nm.

To explored the selectivity of N-CDs to Fe3+, 1 mM of common interfering cations (Cr3+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Al3+) was selected to study the effect of these metal ions on the fluorescence intensity. 2 μL of 0.36 mg/mL N-CDs and 3 mL of 1 mM metal ions was mixed and incubated for 10 min. The fluorescence spectrum intensity of the solutions was recorded under the excitation wavelength (λex) of 320 nm and the emission wavelength (λem) of 430 nm. All the tests were repeated three times.

To evaluate the sensitivity of N-CDs to Fe3+, 2 μL of N-CDs (0.36 mg/mL) was added into 3 mL of 0.01 M acetic acid buffer solution. A series of Fe3+ aqueous solutions with the concentration of 0–100 μM were added into the N-CDs acetic acid buffer solution. The mixture solution was incubated for 15 min at room temperature to obtain the mix solutions with a final N-CDs concentration of 0.24 μg/mL. The fluorescence spectra of all samples were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm and the relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 was calculated as the detection signals for sensing Fe3+, F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively [2]. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated according to the 3σ/s standard formula, where σ is the standard deviation value measured from 10 blank samples, while s represents the slope of the standard curve [33]. All the tests were repeated three times.

To verify the practicability of N-CDs for Fe3+ detection, tap water from Hubei University laboratory and the local sand lake water (Wuhan City) were selected as two actual water samples. The actual water samples were filtered by filter membrane with 0.22 μm to remove solid impurities. Then, the filtered water samples were spiked with Fe3+ to obtain two different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions (20 μM and 50 μM). Finally, N-CDs were added to these solutions and the Fe3+ content in the solutions was calculated by the standard curve, respectively. All the tests were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morpholigy characteristics of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, N-CDs were successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal process. To investigate the structure and morphology of N-CDs, FT-IR, XRD, and TEM instruments were applied. In the FT-IR curve of N-CDs (Fig. 1 a), the absorption peaks at 3450 cm−1, 3220 cm−1, and 1640 cm−1 were assigned to the typical stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H, and C = O, respectively. The peak at 1305 cm−1 was associated with the bending vibration of C–H bond [34,35]. The FT-IR spectroscopy analysis demonstrates that N-CDs surface contains abundant hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl functional groups. A broad diffraction peak near 2θ = 27° was observed in the XRD spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1b), indicating that N-CDs are mainly amorphous carbon structures. As shown in the TEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 1c), the prepared N-CDs exhibited uniform particle size, good dispersibility, and spherical shape [36]. The particle size distribution of sample was in the range of 1.75–3.25 nm, with an average particle size of about 2.58 nm (Fig. 1d).

The chemical composition of the obtained N-CDs was further explored by XPS. The XPS analysis indicated that the composition of N-CDs mainly consisted of carbon (70.09 %), nitrogen (4.31 %), and oxygen (25.60 %) elements, and exhibited the corresponding binding energy at 284.77 eV, 399.01 eV, 532.06 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The deconvolution of the C1s spectrum showed three main peaks at 283.29 eV, 284.82 eV, and 287.24 eV (Fig. 2b), corresponding to the chemical bonds C = C/C–C, C-N/C-O and C = O [37,38]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) at 399.31 eV was attributed to C-N [39]. The O1s spectrum was divided into two peaks at 531.45 eV and 532.81 eV, which was corresponded to the C = O and C-O functional groups (Fig. 2d) [40,41]. The above analysis was consistent with the FT-IR results, indicating the successful preparation of N-CDs containing abundant functional groups. Therefore, the abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of N-CDs endow them with good solubility and dispersibility.

To explore the optical properties of fluorescent N-CDs, the ultraviolet–visible (UV–visible), fluorescence excitation, and fluorescence emission characterizations were investigated. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of N-CDs exhibited two distinct absorption bands (Fig. 3 a). Weak absorption peak at 246 nm was observed, which may originate from the π-π* transition of the C = C bond [42]. Distinct sharp peak at 340 nm was caused by n-π* transition of the carbonyl/amino functional group on the surface of N-CDs [43]. The fluorescence spectra demonstrated that N-CDs exhibited the strongest fluorescence emission at 430 nm under the excitation of 320 nm, and the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ could overlap with the excitation spectra and emission spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3b). The N-CDs aqueous solution showed green and blue under daylight and 365 nm UV light, respectively (Fig. 3b, inset). As the excitation wavelength was gradually increased from 280 nm to 350 nm, the position of the excitation peak remained unchanged, and the excitation intensity showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing (Fig. 3c). The strongest fluorescent intensity of N-CDs was observed when the excitation wavelength at 320 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was derived to be approximately 45 % due to nitrogen doping and surface passivation protection of melamine [44,45].

The stability of fluorescent is critical when evaluating the properties of CDs. To evaluate the fluorescent stability of N-CDs, the influences of UV irradiation, pH, and ion concentration on fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 320 nm were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity exhibited different responses at different pH values (1–13). As the pH value increased from 1 to 9, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased, while the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased when pH was greater than 9. The pH responsiveness of N-CDs may be related to the protonation state of their surface functional groups [46]. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was nearly unchanged in environments of different NaCl concentration of 0–1 mol/L (Fig. 4b) and within 12 h of UV irradiation (Fig. 4c). The above results indicated that N-CDs have excellent salt resistance and high photostability, providing the possibility for detecting Fe3+ in solutions.

The fluorescence response properties of various metal ions were evaluated by N-CDs fluorescent probes (Fig. 5 ). Acetate buffer solution was utilized to control the pH at 7.4 to eliminate the interference of pH fluctuations on selectivity. It can be seen that among all these tested metal ions, only Fe3+ showed the strongest quenching effect, indicating that N-CDs can serve as an efficient fluorescent probe specifically for monitoring Fe3+ in an aqueous solution, and have broad application prospects in practical environments.

The impact of Fe3+ concentrations variations on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was investigated to evaluate the sensitivity of the detection systems. Fig. 6 a showed the changes of the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs after the addition of different concentration (0–100 μM) of Fe3+. As Fe3+ concentration increases, the luminescence intensity of samples gradually decreased. In addition, there was a good linear correlation between the fluorescence response (F/F0) and Fe3+ concentration with the added Fe3+ concentration of 20–80 μM with a linear fitting equation of y = -0.0046x + 0.79127 and a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.98612 (Fig. 6b). It can also be seen from Fig. 6b that with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, the luminescence intensity of the samples showed a consistent linear growth. This indicated that N-CDs have the potential to apply fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+, and the concentration of Fe3+ can be directly estimated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. The low limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was 3.18 μM calculated following the minimum detection limit formula (3σ/k) [47], indicating that N-CDs are suitable for fluorescent detection of Fe3+. The performances of the N-CDs-based Fe3+ fluorescent probes were compared with some of the currently published literatures in Table 1 . Compared with the reported Fe3+ fluorescent probes, our as-prepared N-CDs fluorescent probe have great advantages in simple preparation method, higher quantum yields and can reach lower LOD for detection Fe3+ as the lower N-CDs concentration (0.24 μg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the potential for application of the fluorescent probe N-CDs, their detection properties in actual water samples were evaluated. This method was effectively used to detect Fe3+ content in local sand lake water (Wuhan city, China) and tap water from the laboratory of Hubei University. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery of Fe3+ in the actual samples was ranged from 97.04 % to 100.58 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of Fe3+ determination was between 0.81 % and 1.05 %. These results revealed that the prepared N-CDs can be effectively used for the detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples and serve as a fluorescent probe with high sensitivity and high selectivity toward Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of N-CDs for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The study of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs can maximize the understanding of this phenomenon and provide important references for their development and application. In general, the fluorescence quenching includes two most common types: dynamic quenching and static quenching. When the excited fluorophore group collides with the quencher, dynamic quenching occurs through energy transfer or electron transfer mechanism [48]. The formation of non-fluorescent complexes or chelation between fluorophore with the quenchers is called static quenching, because it occurs in the ground state and does not rely on diffusion or molecular collisions [49]. In order to investigate the detection mechanism of N-CDs on Fe3+, the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 7 a) and fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 7b) before and after quenching were measured. Fig. 7a showed that after adding Fe3+, there was no significant change in the UV–Vis absorption peaks of N-CDs around 246 nm and 320–360 nm. The introduction of Fe3+ did not cause the appearance of new absorption peaks, indicating that N-CDs and Fe3+ did not form new compounds. The changes of the UV–Vis absorption excluded the possibility of inner filter effect [50] and implied that the fluorescence quenching process of Fe3+ on N-CDs may be dynamic quenching process [48–51]. Transient fluorescence spectroscopy can more accurately identify the fluorescence lifetime before and after quenching, and is considered the most prevalent means for checking the quenching process [48,49]. Fig. 7b exhibited the transient fluorescence spectroscopy of N-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+. The presence of Fe3+ decreased the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs solution. For interpretation of the data obtained here shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence decay curves were fitted with double-exponential function [52]. Based on the fitting results, the average decay times of N-CDs in absence and presence of 40 μM Fe3+were calculated to be 7.80 ns and 5.19 ns, respectively. Such a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime further indicated that the quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ was dynamic quenching [53]. According to the above analysis in Fig. 3b, the absorption band of Fe3+ exhibited significant spectral overlap with the excitation and emission peaks of N-CDs, suggested that the fluorescence of N-CDs might be quenched by Fe3+ due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [26]. Based on above analysis, the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ was ascribed to FRET and the quenching mechanism was described in Fig. 7c.

In this work, N-CDs were successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) as carbon source, and melamine as nitrogen source, respectively. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited excitation-independent properties, good photostability, and excellent fluorescence performances with a quantum yield of up to 45 % and an average fluorescence lifetime of about 7.80 ns. The fluorescence N-CDs can be served as fluorescent probes for the detecting Fe3+ with high sensitivity and good selectivity under low-concentration of 0.24 μg/mL. The low limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was 3.18 μM in the linear detection range of 20–80 μM. Furthermore, the developed fluorescent probe can be applied in real environmental water samples with the recoveries of 97.04 %-100.58 %. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was reduced under the influence of Fe3+, which was ascribed to the FRET mechanism. The prepared N-CDs provide a new strategy for detecting metal ions using low-concentration fluorescent probes.

Yulin Ma: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Linhan Mao: Resources, Formal analysis. Congcong Cui: Software, Formal analysis. Yong Hu: Validation, Funding acquisition. Zhaoxia Chen: Supervision, Methodology. Yuan Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yuhong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021CFB025) and Department of Education of Hubei Province, China (Q20221010).
